name: Dask
import_as:
import dask.dataframe as dd
import dask.array as da
summary: Parallel and out-of-core computing for Python. Provides lazy pandas and NumPy-compatible APIs that scale from a single machine to a cluster without rewriting code.

tasks.task: Read and process datasets too large for RAM
tasks.mental_trigger: My CSV or Parquet dataset doesn't fit in memory and pandas read_csv runs OOM
important_params: filepath — glob pattern like 'data-*.csv' to read multiple files as partitions
blocksize — size of each partition in bytes e.g. '128MB'; tune to worker memory
dtype — dict of column types to prevent inference errors across partitions
tasks.example:
import dask.dataframe as dd

ddf = dd.read_csv('data-*.csv', dtype={'score': float, 'label': int})

# Operations are lazy until .compute()
result = ddf[ddf['score'] > 0.8]['label'].value_counts().compute()
tasks.use_when: Files that exceed RAM. Multi-file glob processing. When you want pandas-compatible API without loading everything into memory.
tasks.avoid_when: Data fits in memory — pandas is faster for in-memory work because it has no partitioning overhead. For analytical workloads on very large files, consider Polars with streaming instead.
tasks.decision_notes: Dask operations are entirely lazy. Nothing executes until .compute() is called. Forgetting .compute() is the single most common mistake — you get a Dask object back, not data.
gotchas: All operations return lazy Dask objects; forgetting .compute() silently returns a task graph object that passes type checks but contains no actual data.
Column assignment (ddf['new'] = series) is not supported in Dask DataFrames — use ddf.assign(new=expr) or map_partitions.

tasks.task: Parallelize NumPy array operations across chunks
tasks.mental_trigger: I have a massive NumPy array or need to parallelize reduction/matrix ops across multiple CPU cores
tasks.syntax:
da.from_array(numpy_arr, chunks=chunk_size)
da.random.random(size, chunks=chunk_size)
da.sum(arr, axis)
result = computation.compute()
important_params: chunks — size of each chunk per dimension e.g. (1000, 1000) or '128MB'
rechunk(chunks) — repartition after loading if chunk sizes are irregular
tasks.example:
import dask.array as da
import numpy as np

np_arr = np.random.rand(100_000, 1000)
da_arr = da.from_array(np_arr, chunks=(10_000, 1000))  # 10 partitions along axis 0

mean_per_col = da.mean(da_arr, axis=0).compute()       # parallel reduction
tasks.use_when: NumPy reductions or element-wise ops on arrays that exceed RAM, or when you want to use all CPU cores for embarrassingly parallel array operations.
tasks.avoid_when: Chunks smaller than 1MB — task overhead dominates. Highly irregular access patterns like fancy indexing — rechunking will kill performance.
tasks.decision_notes: Chunk size is the most critical tuning parameter. Target 100MB per chunk for CPU workers, 500MB–1GB per chunk for I/O-bound work. Use rechunk() to normalize after loading.
gotchas: Chunks smaller than 1MB create excessive task overhead and are slower than single-threaded NumPy.
Mixed chunk sizes along an axis prevent fusion of reduction operations — always rechunk to uniform sizes before sum or mean.

tasks.task: Wrap arbitrary Python functions for parallel execution
tasks.mental_trigger: I have a plain Python function I want to run in parallel over a list of inputs without writing multiprocessing code
important_params: pure=True — tells Dask the function has no side effects, enabling caching
tasks.use_when: Parallelizing existing functions over lists of inputs. Processing a directory of files. Running embarrassingly parallel preprocessing pipelines.
tasks.avoid_when: Functions with shared mutable state, database connections, or GPU resources — these do not serialize cleanly across Dask workers.
tasks.decision_notes: dask.delayed is the lowest-friction way to parallelize arbitrary Python code. For data that fits in pandas/NumPy, it outperforms multiprocessing.Pool because Dask avoids repeated process spawn overhead.
gotchas: dask.delayed functions must be serializable — closures that capture large objects or database connections fail at worker spawn time.
Using the synchronous scheduler (default in notebooks) runs tasks sequentially — switch to 'threads' or 'processes' for actual parallelism.
